note: Amina is a 9-year-old girl who entered the system following neglect proceedings. She speaks both Somali and English fluently and feels most comfortable in a bilingual environment. Amina follows a Halal diet strictly and this is non-negotiable per family religious practice. She has been diagnosed with generalized anxiety and her therapist recommends weekly sessions to continue — disrupting this care would be significantly harmful. She is currently enrolled in 3rd grade at Jefferson Elementary in the SE district and her teacher reports she is doing well socially; maintaining her school placement would support her stability. Amina has no siblings in the system. She enjoys drawing and is described by her caseworker as gentle and observant. She has had one prior placement that disrupted due to dietary needs not being met.

Marcus is a 14-year-old male with a history of behavioral incidents at school and two prior placement disruptions. He has been diagnosed with PTSD following domestic violence exposure and requires a trauma-informed household. His older brother (17) is placed separately and they maintain monthly supervised contact. Marcus speaks English only. He does not have specific dietary requirements. His therapist has flagged that consistency and clear boundaries are critical — a home with experience managing behavioral needs is strongly preferred. Marcus is currently not enrolled in school and re-enrollment in any district is acceptable. He is described as intelligent and creative but distrustful of adults initially. A patient, experienced placement is essential.

Sofia and Lucia are 6-year-old twin sisters who must be placed together — separation is not an option. They are Spanish-speaking with limited English and a Spanish-speaking household is strongly preferred to ease their transition. Sofia has mild asthma and requires access to a nebulizer and should not be in a home with pets or smokers. Lucia has no medical needs. Both girls are described as affectionate and bonded to each other. They have no prior placements. Standard diet, no religious restrictions. A home with capacity for two children and ideally in a district with ESL support would be ideal. Their caseworker notes that stability and warmth are the top priorities for this placement.
